skill.name: graphql
skill.description: Guidance for designing, implementing, and reviewing GraphQL APIs with safer schemas, efficient resolvers, DataLoader batching, federation, subscriptions, and client integration.
skill.summary: Build GraphQL APIs with careful schema design, resolver safety, batching, and production controls.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported shell backticks and weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but review found no executable code, scripts, network calls, or cryptographic operations. The backticks are Markdown formatting on line 68, and the weak-crypto hits are text matches in GraphQL and design-related prose. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at line 68. The line only uses Markdown inline code formatting for related skill names, so no command execution path is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line contains only Markdown text and inline backticks around skill names. There is no script language context or executable command syntax.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Design Text Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns on lines 3, 21, 33, and 62. These lines contain GraphQL description and schema design text, not cryptographic APIs or algorithm selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are natural-language GraphQL guidance and capability names. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic implementation appears in the file.

content.value_statement: GraphQL APIs can become slow, unclear, or unsafe when schemas and resolvers grow without controls. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide schema design, resolver patterns, DataLoader usage, federation, subscriptions, and client integration.
actual_capabilities: Guides GraphQL schema design with type safety and intentional nullability.
Explains resolver patterns that reduce authorization and data loading mistakes.
Recommends DataLoader batching to prevent N+1 database queries.
Covers Apollo Server, Apollo Client, urql, federation, subscriptions, and code generation.
Highlights production controls such as query depth, query cost, and introspection settings.
limitations: Does not include runnable GraphQL server templates or generated project files.
Does not execute tests, migrations, or schema validation by itself.
Does not replace framework-specific security review for production deployments.
Provides guidance only for the patterns documented in the single skill file.

use_cases.title: Design a New GraphQL Schema
use_cases.description: Plan object types, fields, nullability, and relationships before implementation.

use_cases.title: Review Resolver Performance
use_cases.description: Find N+1 query risks and decide where DataLoader batching should be added.

use_cases.title: Prepare Production GraphQL Controls
use_cases.description: Check depth limits, query cost rules, introspection settings, and field authorization.

prompt_templates.title: Explain My Schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an existing schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this GraphQL schema for unclear types, nullability mistakes, and missing relationships. Explain the most important changes first.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Resolver Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these GraphQL resolvers for N+1 query risks. Suggest where DataLoader batching and caching should be introduced.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Production GraphQL
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this GraphQL API design for query depth, query cost, introspection, authorization, and subscription cleanup risks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Federation Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Help design GraphQL federation boundaries for these services. Identify entity ownership, resolver responsibilities, and schema contract risks.

output_examples.input: Review a user and orders schema for production readiness.
output_examples.output: The review identifies unclear nullability, missing field-level authorization, and a likely N+1 query path from users to orders.

output_examples.input: Improve resolvers that fetch related records one item at a time.
output_examples.output: The response recommends batching related lookups with DataLoader and separating per-request cache scope from shared application state.

output_examples.input: Prepare an Apollo API for public clients.
output_examples.output: The guidance covers query depth limits, cost analysis, disabled production introspection, resolver authorization, and monitoring for slow operations.

best_practices: Design nullability intentionally so clients can distinguish missing data from failed resolution.
Use DataLoader or equivalent batching for resolver paths that load related records.
Apply authorization in resolvers and fields, not only in schema directives.
anti_patterns: Allowing unlimited query depth or complexity for public clients.
Letting every resolver make independent database calls for related data.
Keeping production introspection enabled without a clear access control policy.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete GraphQL server?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance and review prompts, but it does not include a full server scaffold.

faq.question: Which GraphQL tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It covers Apollo Server, Apollo Client, urql, DataLoader, GraphQL code generation, federation, and subscriptions.

faq.question: Can it help with GraphQL performance problems?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on N+1 query prevention, batching, caching, and query cost controls.

faq.question: Does it cover GraphQL security?
faq.answer: Yes. It highlights query depth limits, complexity controls, introspection settings, and resolver-level authorization.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found only Markdown guidance and no executable code, network access, or prompt injection content.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Backend engineers, API maintainers, and platform teams working on GraphQL schema and resolver quality.
